draft.introduction: The Code of Ur-Nammu (c. 2100–2050 BCE) is the oldest surviving legal code in human history, predating the more famous Code of Hammurabi by roughly three centuries. Composed in Sumerian during the Third Dynasty of Ur, the code survives in fragmentary form on clay tablets discovered at Nippur. Its prologue establishes the king’s authority as divinely granted and frames justice as a cosmic obligation—the king acts as the earthly representative of the gods Nanna and Utu. The theological significance is considerable. The prologue presents law not as human invention but as divine order mediated through kingship. The concept of cosmic justice (nig-si-sa) that pervades the code is the Sumerian ancestor of later Near Eastern ideas about divine law—ideas that flow directly into the Torah, the Quranic shari’a, and the Greek concept of dike. The specific laws, with their preference for fines over corporal punishment, reveal a surprisingly humane legal philosophy.

translation.text: After An and Enlil had turned over the kingship of Ur to Nanna, at that time Ur-Nammu, son born of the goddess Ninsun, for his beloved mother who bore him—in accordance with the just laws of Utu, he established equity in the land. He expelled the dishonest, the grabbers, and the oppressors. He established the nig-si-sa (cosmic justice) in the land.
translation.original_text: 𒀀 𒌔𒃠𒂋 𒂫𒀑𒁎 𒂋𒁗𒂋 𒀑𒃵𒁎 𒁲𒂫 𒁳𒂋𒁗𒂫 𒂋𒁉𒂋 𒃡𒀒

translation.text: He made the silver mina weigh sixty shekels. He made the shekel of silver correspond to the shekel of grain. The orphan was not delivered up to the rich man. The widow was not delivered up to the powerful man. The man of one shekel was not delivered up to the man of one mina.

If a man commits murder, that man shall be killed. If a man commits robbery, that man shall be killed. If a man detains another without lawful cause, that man shall be imprisoned and shall pay fifteen shekels of silver. If a man divorces his first wife, he shall pay her one mina of silver.

If a man cuts off the foot of another man, he shall pay ten shekels of silver. If a man shatters the bone of another man with a club, he shall pay one mina of silver. If a man cuts off the nose of another man with a copper knife, he shall pay two-thirds of a mina of silver.

translator_notes.note: The concept of nig-si-sa (literally “the thing that is straight”) is the Sumerian equivalent of cosmic justice or right order. It is not merely human fairness but a metaphysical principle: the universe has a moral structure, and the king’s role is to align human society with it. This concept flows into the Akkadian kittum and misharum, the Egyptian ma’at, and ultimately into the Hebrew tsedek.

The preference for monetary compensation over bodily punishment (“an eye for an eye”) is striking. Hammurabi’s later code, written for a more stratified society, prescribes talion (like-for-like punishment) in many cases where Ur-Nammu prescribes fines. This suggests that the oldest known legal tradition was in some respects more humane than its successors.
